Additionally, following the closure of Russian airspace in 2022, multiple airlines ceased operations in Hong Kong.[[266]](#cite_note-273) In an attempt to attract tourists back to Hong Kong, the Hong Kong government announced plans to give away 500,000 free airline tickets in 2023.[[267]](#cite_note-274) Hong Kong was [the most visited city internationally](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_international_visitors "List of cities by international visitors") in the Asia-Pacific region in 2023, with over 2 million more international visitors than its closest competitor, [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok").[[268]](#cite_note-275)

Well-known Hong Kong landmarks include the [Victoria Peak](/wiki/Victoria_Peak "Victoria Peak"), the iconic [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") skyline, and [The Big Buddha (Hong Kong)](/wiki/The_Big_Buddha_(Hong_Kong) "The Big Buddha (Hong Kong)"). Other iconic sites include the historic [Star Ferry](/wiki/Star_Ferry "Star Ferry"), [Avenue of Stars, Hong Kong](/wiki/Avenue_of_Stars,_Hong_Kong "Avenue of Stars, Hong Kong"), [Man Mo Temple](/wiki/Man_Mo_Temple "Man Mo Temple"), [Hong Kong UNESCO Global Geopark](/wiki/Hong_Kong_UNESCO_Global_Geopark "Hong Kong UNESCO Global Geopark") in [Sai Kung District](/wiki/Sai_Kung_District "Sai Kung District") and [Lamma Island](/wiki/Lamma_Island "Lamma Island").

Luxury hotels in Hong Kong include the [Rosewood Hong Kong](/wiki/Rosewood_Hong_Kong "Rosewood Hong Kong") (#1 World's 50 Best Hotels), [Upper House Hong Kong](/wiki/Upper_House_Hong_Kong "Upper House Hong Kong") (#10 World's 50 Best Hotels), [Mandarin Oriental Hong Kong](/wiki/Mandarin_Oriental_Hong_Kong "Mandarin Oriental Hong Kong"), [The Peninsula Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Peninsula_Hong_Kong "The Peninsula Hong Kong"), [Four Seasons Hotel Hong Kong](/wiki/Four_Seasons_Hotel_Hong_Kong "Four Seasons Hotel Hong Kong"), [Island Shangri-La](/wiki/Island_Shangri-La "Island Shangri-La") and the [Ritz-Carlton Hong Kong](/wiki/Ritz-Carlton_Hong_Kong "Ritz-Carlton Hong Kong") (world's highest hotel).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Hong_Kong_Skyline_view_from_the_peak_2017.jpg/960px-Hong_Kong_Skyline_view_from_the_peak_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hong_Kong_Skyline_view_from_the_peak_2017.jpg "Victoria Peak is a major tourist attraction that offers views of Central and Victoria Harbour.")

[Victoria Peak](/wiki/Victoria_Peak "Victoria Peak") is a major tourist attraction that offers views of [Central](/wiki/Central,_Hong_Kong "Central, Hong Kong") and [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour").

## Infrastructure

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Hong Kong](/wiki/Transport_in_Hong_Kong "Transport in Hong Kong")

[![Tunnel entrance at night, with heavy traffic](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Cross_Harbour_Tunnel_%281%29.JPG/250px-Cross_Harbour_Tunnel_%281%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cross_Harbour_Tunnel_(1).JPG)

Entrance to the [Cross-Harbour Tunnel](/wiki/Cross-Harbour_Tunnel "Cross-Harbour Tunnel") in [Hung Hom](/wiki/Hung_Hom "Hung Hom"), [Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon "Kowloon")

Hong Kong has a highly developed and sophisticated transport network. Over 90% of daily trips made by its residents are made with public transport, the highest percentage in the world.[[269]](#cite_note-pubtransport-276) The [Octopus card](/wiki/Octopus_card "Octopus card"), a [contactless](/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment") [smart](/wiki/Smart_card "Smart card") [payment](/wiki/Digital_currency "Digital currency") card made for Hong Kong, is widely accepted on railways, trams, buses and ferries, and can also be used for payment in most retail stores.[[270]](#cite_note-277) Alternative payments such as [Apple Pay](/wiki/Apple_Pay "Apple Pay"), [AliPay](/wiki/Alipay "Alipay"), [Mastercard](/wiki/Mastercard "Mastercard") and [Visa](/wiki/Visa_Inc. "Visa Inc.") have also been gradually introduced as methods of payment for public transport.[[271]](#cite_note-278)[[272]](#cite_note-279)[[273]](#cite_note-280)[[274]](#cite_note-281)[[275]](#cite_note-282)

Hong Kong's EV policy, driven by the "Hong Kong Roadmap on Popularisation of Electric Vehicles," aims to achieve zero vehicular emissions before 2050. Key strategies include banning new registration of fuel-propelled private cars, including hybrids, by 2035 or earlier, expanding charging infrastructure, and offering significant, extended tax concessions such as the One-for-One Replacement Scheme (expiring 31 March 2026).[[276]](#cite_note-283)

### Land

The [Peak Tram](/wiki/Peak_Tram "Peak Tram"), Hong Kong's first public transport system, has provided [funicular](/wiki/Funicular "Funicular") rail transport between [Central](/wiki/Central,_Hong_Kong "Central, Hong Kong") and [Victoria Peak](/wiki/Victoria_Peak "Victoria Peak") since 1888.[[277]](#cite_note-284)

The [Central and Western District](/wiki/Central_and_Western_District "Central and Western District") has an extensive system of escalators and [moving pavements](/wiki/Moving_walkway "Moving walkway"), which, together being the [Mid-Levels escalator system](/wiki/Central%E2%80%93Mid-Levels_escalator "Central–Mid-Levels escalator"), is the world's longest outdoor covered escalator system.[[278]](#cite_note-285)

[Hong Kong Tramways](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Tramways "Hong Kong Tramways")’ tram network covers a portion of Hong Kong Island, covering from [Kennedy Town](/wiki/Kennedy_Town "Kennedy Town") to [Shau Kei Wan](/wiki/Shau_Kei_Wan "Shau Kei Wan"), with a branch to [Happy Valley](/wiki/Happy_Valley,_Hong_Kong "Happy Valley, Hong Kong"). It operates 6 routes and has had a ridership of 42,558 in 2022.[[279]](#cite_note-286)[[280]](#cite_note-287) It began servicing Hong Kong since 1904.[[281]](#cite_note-288) Hong Kong Tramways currently holds the Guinness World Record as the "Largest double-decker tram fleet in service", certified on 30 July 2021, with a fleet of 165 double-decker trams.[[282]](#cite_note-289) The fleet of trams was mostly built by Hong Kong Tramways.

The [Mass Transit Railway](/wiki/MTR "MTR") (MTR) is an extensive passenger rail network, connecting 99 [metro](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") stations and 68 [light-rail](/wiki/Light_Rail_(MTR) "Light Rail (MTR)") stops throughout the territory.[[283]](#cite_note-RailwayFacts-290)[[284]](#cite_note-291) With a daily ridership of almost five million, the system serves 41% of all public transit passengers in the city[[285]](#cite_note-LegcoTransport-292) and has an on-time rate of 99.9%.[[286]](#cite_note-293)

Train service to [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") is offered by the [East Rail line](/wiki/East_Rail_line "East Rail line"), and longer-distance [inter-city trains](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") [to Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou%E2%80%93Kowloon_through_train "Guangzhou–Kowloon through train"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Kowloon_through_train "Shanghai–Kowloon through train"), [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Kowloon_through_train "Beijing–Kowloon through train") are operated from [Hung Hom station](/wiki/Hung_Hom_station "Hung Hom station").[[287]](#cite_note-294) This train service has however been suspended since the [outbreak of COVID-19](/wiki/Outbreak_of_COVID-19 "Outbreak of COVID-19"), and officially closed by the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China") on 31 July 2024, as announced by the [General Administration of Customs](/wiki/General_Administration_of_Customs "General Administration of Customs"), citing that [high-speed passenger trains](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Guangzhou_high-speed_railway "Beijing–Guangzhou high-speed railway") have effectively met the travel needs of passengers between the mainland and Hong Kong.[[288]](#cite_note-295)[[289]](#cite_note-296)[[290]](#cite_note-297) [Connecting service](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Express_Rail_Link "Hong Kong Express Rail Link") to the [national high-speed rail system](/wiki/China_Railway_High-speed "China Railway High-speed") is provided at [West Kowloon railway station](/wiki/Hong_Kong_West_Kowloon_railway_station "Hong Kong West Kowloon railway station").[[291]](#cite_note-298)

Although public transport systems handle most passenger traffic, over 500,000 private vehicles are registered in Hong Kong.[[292]](#cite_note-299) Unlike in mainland China, automobiles in Hong Kong drive [on the left](/wiki/Left-_and_right-hand_traffic "Left- and right-hand traffic") due to the historical influence of the British Empire.[[293]](#cite_note-300) Vehicle traffic is extremely congested in urban areas, exacerbated by limited space to expand roads and an increasing number of vehicles.[[294]](#cite_note-301) More than 18,000 [taxicabs](/wiki/Taxis_of_Hong_Kong "Taxis of Hong Kong"), easily identifiable by their bright colours and taxi lights, are licensed to carry riders in the territory.[[295]](#cite_note-302) Unlicensed ride-hailing services such as [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber") exist in Hong Kong as well, with some operating with licensed taxis to legitimise their business in ride-sharing. Unlicensed drivers have been targeted by the government and taxi drivers in the past, mainly due to the lack of [third-party insurance](/wiki/Third-party_insurance "Third-party insurance") for passengers, and because taxi drivers feared competition from drivers of these ride-hailing services.[[296]](#cite_note-303)[[297]](#cite_note-304) In 2024, the government looked into legalising these services.[[298]](#cite_note-305)

[Bus services](/wiki/Bus_services_in_Hong_Kong "Bus services in Hong Kong") operate more than 700 routes across the territory,[[285]](#cite_note-LegcoTransport-292) with smaller [public light buses](/wiki/Public_light_bus "Public light bus") (also known as minibuses) serving areas standard buses do not reach as frequently or directly.[[299]](#cite_note-306) Expressways and truck roads, organised with the [Hong Kong Strategic Route and Exit Number System](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Strategic_Route_and_Exit_Number_System "Hong Kong Strategic Route and Exit Number System"), connect all major areas of the territory.[[300]](#cite_note-307) The [Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge](/wiki/Hong_Kong%E2%80%93Zhuhai%E2%80%93Macau_Bridge "Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge") provides a direct route to the western side of the Pearl River estuary.[[248]](#cite_note-HZMBridge-255)

[![Light-rail train on a straightaway](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/V813_entering_Sunny_Bay_Station_%2820181013082946%29.jpg/250px-V813_entering_Sunny_Bay_Station_%2820181013082946%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:V813_entering_Sunny_Bay_Station_(20181013082946).jpg)

MTR train on the [Tung Chung line](/wiki/Tung_Chung_line "Tung Chung line")

### Air

[Hong Kong International Airport](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport") is the territory's primary airport, replacing [Kai Tak International Airport](/wiki/Kai_Tak_Airport "Kai Tak Airport") which ended operations in 1998.[[301]](#cite_note-308)

Over 100 airlines operate flights from the airport, including [Cathay Pacific](/wiki/Cathay_Pacific "Cathay Pacific"), the [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") of Hong Kong. It has been regarded as a 5-star airline by Skytrax, and been in the world top 10 list of airlines for multiple consecutive years. [Hong Kong Airlines](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Airlines "Hong Kong Airlines"), low-cost airline [HK Express](/wiki/HK_Express "HK Express"), low-cost airline [Greater Bay Airlines](/wiki/Greater_Bay_Airlines "Greater Bay Airlines"), and cargo airline [Air Hong Kong](/wiki/Air_Hong_Kong "Air Hong Kong") are all airlines based in Hong Kong.[[302]](#cite_note-309) It was the [eighth-busiest airport by passenger traffic](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic")[[303]](#cite_note-310) pre-COVID and handles [the most air-cargo traffic in the world](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_cargo_traffic "List of busiest airports by cargo traffic").[[304]](#cite_note-311) Most private recreational aviation traffic flies through [Shek Kong Airfield](/wiki/Shek_Kong_Airfield "Shek Kong Airfield"), under the supervision of the [Hong Kong Aviation Club](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Aviation_Club "Hong Kong Aviation Club").[[305]](#cite_note-312)

### Sea

The [Port of Hong Kong](/wiki/Port_of_Hong_Kong "Port of Hong Kong") located by the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"), is a deepwater [seaport](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") dominated by trade in [containerised](/wiki/Containerisation "Containerisation") manufactured products. A key factor in the economic development of Hong Kong, the natural shelter and deep waters of [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour "Victoria Harbour") provide ideal conditions for berthing and the handling of all types of vessels. It is one of the [busiest ports](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_port "World's busiest port") in the world, in the three categories of shipping movements, cargo handled and passengers carried. This makes Hong Kong a Large-Port Metropolis.[[306]](#cite_note-313)

The [Star Ferry](/wiki/Star_Ferry "Star Ferry") operates two lines across Victoria Harbour for its 53,000 daily passengers.[[307]](#cite_note-314) Ferries also serve outlying islands inaccessible by other means. Smaller [kai-to](/wiki/Kai-to "Kai-to") boats serve the most remote coastal settlements.[[308]](#cite_note-315) Ferry travel to Macau and mainland China is also available.[[309]](#cite_note-316) [Junks](/wiki/Junk_(ship) "Junk (ship)"), once common in Hong Kong waters, are no longer widely available and are used privately and for tourism.[[310]](#cite_note-317)
The large size of the port gives Hong Kong the classification of Large-Port Metropolis.[[311]](#cite_note-318)

### Utilities

Main articles: [Energy in Hong Kong](/wiki/Energy_in_Hong_Kong "Energy in Hong Kong"), [Electricity sector in Hong Kong](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Hong_Kong "Electricity sector in Hong Kong"), and [Water supply and sanitation in Hong Kong](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Hong_Kong "Water supply and sanitation in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Lamma_Power_Station_-_October_2019.jpg/250px-Lamma_Power_Station_-_October_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lamma_Power_Station_-_October_2019.jpg)

[Lamma Power Station](/wiki/Lamma_Power_Station "Lamma Power Station"), a coal power plant on [Lamma Island](/wiki/Lamma_Island "Lamma Island") owned by [Hongkong Electric](/wiki/Hongkong_Electric_Company "Hongkong Electric Company")

Hong Kong generates most of its electricity locally.[[312]](#cite_note-energystats2016p1-319) The vast majority of this energy comes from fossil fuels, with 46% from coal and 47% from petroleum.[[313]](#cite_note-320) The rest is from other imports, including nuclear energy generated in mainland China.[[314]](#cite_note-321) Renewable sources account for a negligible amount of energy generated for the territory.[[315]](#cite_note-322) Small-scale wind-power sources have been developed,[[312]](#cite_note-energystats2016p1-319) and a small number of private homes and public buildings have installed solar panels.[[316]](#cite_note-323)

With few natural lakes and rivers, high population density, inaccessible groundwater sources, and extremely seasonal rainfall, the territory lacks a reliable freshwater supply. The [Dong River](/wiki/Dong_River_(China) "Dong River (China)") in Guangdong supplies 70% of the city's water,[[317]](#cite_note-LeeWater-324) with the remaining demand met by harvesting rainwater locally.[[318]](#cite_note-325) Toilets in most built-up areas of the territory flush with seawater, which reduces freshwater use.[[317]](#cite_note-LeeWater-324)

[Broadband](/wiki/Broadband "Broadband") Internet access is widely available, with 92.6% of households connected. Connections over [fibre-optic infrastructure](/wiki/Fiber_to_the_x "Fiber to the x") are increasingly prevalent,[[319]](#cite_note-326) contributing to the high regional average connection speed of 21.9 Mbit/s (the world's fourth-fastest).[[320]](#cite_note-327) Mobile-phone use is ubiquitous;[[321]](#cite_note-328) there are almost 22 million [mobile-phone accounts](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_mobile_phones_in_use "List of countries by number of mobile phones in use") registered in Hong Kong,[[322]](#cite_note-329) which is almost triple the territory's population.

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Hong Kong](/wiki/Culture_of_Hong_Kong "Culture of Hong Kong")

See also: [Cantonese culture](/wiki/Cantonese_culture "Cantonese culture")

Hong Kong culture is characterised as a hybrid of [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_world "Eastern world") and [Western](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") culture. Traditional Chinese values emphasising family and education blend with Western ideals, including economic liberty and the rule of law.[[323]](#cite_note-330) Although the vast majority of the population is ethnically Chinese, Hong Kong has developed a distinct identity. The territory diverged from the mainland through its long period of colonial administration and a different pace of economic, social, and cultural development. Mainstream culture was derived from immigrants originating from various parts of China; it was then influenced by British-style education, a separate political system, and the territory's rapid development during the late 20th century.[[324]](#cite_note-331)[[325]](#cite_note-332) Most migrants of that era fled poverty and war, reflected in the prevailing attitude toward wealth; Hongkongers would tend to link self-image and decision-making to material benefits.[[326]](#cite_note-333)[[327]](#cite_note-334) Residents' sense of local identity has increased post-handover: polling in December 2022 had 32% of respondents identifying as "Hongkongers", 34.1% identifying as "Hongkongers in China" 45.9% purporting a "Mixed Identity", 20.5% identifying as "Chinese" and 11.9% identifying as "Chinese in Hong Kong".[[328]](#cite_note-335)

Traditional Chinese family values, including [family honour](/wiki/Family_honor "Family honor"), [filial piety](/wiki/Filial_piety "Filial piety"), and a [preference for sons](/wiki/Sex_selection "Sex selection"), are prevalent.[[329]](#cite_note-336) [Nuclear families](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") are the most common households, although multi-generational and extended families are not unusual.[[330]](#cite_note-337) Spiritual concepts such as *[feng shui](/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui")* are observed; large-scale construction projects often hire consultants to ensure proper building positioning and layout. The degree of its adherence to *feng shui* is believed to determine a business's success.[[193]](#cite_note-FengShui-200) *[Bagua](/wiki/Bagua "Bagua")* mirrors are regularly used to deflect evil spirits,[[331]](#cite_note-338) and buildings often lack [floor numbers with a 4](/wiki/Chinese_numerology "Chinese numerology");[[332]](#cite_note-339) the number has a similar sound to the word for "die" in Cantonese.[[333]](#cite_note-340)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Hong Kong cuisine](/wiki/Hong_Kong_cuisine "Hong Kong cuisine")

[![An assortment of items in a Dim Sum breakfast meal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Dim_Sum_Breakfast.jpg/250px-Dim_Sum_Breakfast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dim_Sum_Breakfast.jpg)

[![French Toast on left, Milk Tea on right](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Cha_Chaan_Teng.jpg/250px-Cha_Chaan_Teng.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cha_Chaan_Teng.jpg)

Typical fare at a dim sum restaurant (left); *[cha chaan teng](/wiki/Cha_chaan_teng "Cha chaan teng")* breakfast food with [Hong Kong-style milk tea](/wiki/Hong_Kong-style_milk_tea "Hong Kong-style milk tea") (right)

Food in Hong Kong is primarily based on [Cantonese cuisine](/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine "Cantonese cuisine"), despite the territory's exposure to foreign influences and its residents' varied origins. Rice is the staple food, and is usually served plain with other dishes.[[334]](#cite_note-LongHKFood-341) Freshness of ingredients is emphasised. Poultry and seafood are commonly sold live at [wet markets](/wiki/Wet_market "Wet market"), and ingredients are used as quickly as possible when still fresh.[[335]](#cite_note-342) There are up to five daily meals: breakfast, lunch, afternoon tea, dinner, and *[siu yeh](/wiki/Siu_yeh "Siu yeh")*.[[336]](#cite_note-343) [Dim sum](/wiki/Dim_sum "Dim sum"), as part of *[yum cha](/wiki/Yum_cha "Yum cha")* (brunch), is a dining-out tradition with family and friends. Dishes include [congee](/wiki/Congee "Congee"), *[cha siu bao](/wiki/Cha_siu_bao "Cha siu bao")*, *[siu yuk](/wiki/Siu_yuk "Siu yuk")*, [egg tarts](/wiki/Egg_tart "Egg tart"), and [mango pudding](/wiki/Mango_pudding "Mango pudding"). Local versions of Western food are served at *[cha chaan teng](/wiki/Cha_chaan_teng "Cha chaan teng")* (Hong Kong-style cafes). Common *cha chaan teng* menu items include macaroni in soup, deep-fried French toast, and [Hong Kong-style milk tea](/wiki/Hong_Kong-style_milk_tea "Hong Kong-style milk tea").[[334]](#cite_note-LongHKFood-341)

As of 2025, the [Michelin](/wiki/Michelin_guide "Michelin guide") Guide listed 76 starred restaurants in total. It awarded 7 restaurants 3 Michelin Stars, 11 restaurants with 2 Michelin Stars, and 58 restaurants with 1 Michelin Star. It also gave 4 Green Stars for sustainability, Amber, Feuille, Mora, and Roganic. Alongside the starred list, 65 restaurants received [Bib Gourmand](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide") status for good quality food at good value.

### Language

Main article: [Languages of Hong Kong](/wiki/Languages_of_Hong_Kong "Languages of Hong Kong")

The predominant language today is [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"), a [variety of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") originating in Guangzhou. It is spoken by 93.7% of the population, 88.2% as a first language and 5.5% as a second language.[[3]](#cite_note-2021By-CensusLanguages-3) Slightly over half the population (58.7%) speaks [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), the other official language;[[2]](#cite_note-OfficialLanguagesOrd-2) 4.6% are native speakers, and 54.1% speak English as a second language.[[3]](#cite_note-2021By-CensusLanguages-3) [Code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching_in_Hong_Kong "Code-switching in Hong Kong"), mixing English and Cantonese in informal conversation, is common among the bilingual population.[[337]](#cite_note-344) Post-handover governments have promoted [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"), which is currently about as prevalent as English; 54.2% of the population speaks Mandarin, with 2.3% native speakers and 51.9% as a second language.[[3]](#cite_note-2021By-CensusLanguages-3) [Traditional Chinese characters](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters") are used in writing, rather than the [simplified characters](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") used in the [mainland](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China").[[338]](#cite_note-345)

Before the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), Hong Kong had no significant [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese")-speaking population. Instead, most people spoke [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") varieties. However, large areas with [Weitou dialect](/wiki/Weitou_dialect "Weitou dialect") and [Tanka Cantonese](/wiki/Tanka_dialect "Tanka dialect") speakers can be found in the northern New Territories and southern coastal areas, respectively. [Hong Kong Hakka](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Hakka "Hong Kong Hakka") is a variety of Neo-Hakka, and belongs to the [Mei-Hui Hakka](/w/index.php?title=Mei-Hui_Hakka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mei-Hui Hakka (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%A2%85%E6%83%A0%E5%B0%8F%E7%89%87 "zh:梅惠小片")] subbranch of [Yuetai Hakka](/w/index.php?title=Yuetai_Hakka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yuetai Hakka (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%B2%B5%E5%8F%B0%E5%AE%A2%E8%AA%9E "zh:粵台客語")], making it closely related to [Meixian dialect](/wiki/Meixian_dialect "Meixian dialect").[[339]](#cite_note-346) Weitou dialect is a variety that is closely related to the [Yue Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") varieties spoken in [Bao'an](/wiki/Bao%27an,_Shenzhen "Bao'an, Shenzhen") and [Dongguan](/wiki/Dongguan "Dongguan"), and was the primary language of the [Five Great Clans of the New Territories](/wiki/Five_Great_Clans_of_the_New_Territories "Five Great Clans of the New Territories"), brought into Hong Kong during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") from [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi").[[340]](#cite_note-distributionNT-347) Some of the native Yue Chinese varieties were noticeably Hakka-influenced.[[341]](#cite_note-348) Smaller communities of [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") speakers also existed, and many villages were multicultural.[[340]](#cite_note-distributionNT-347)

Today, communities of speakers of other Chinese varieties (such as [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_Min "Teochew Min"), [Sze Yap](/wiki/Siyi_Yue "Siyi Yue"), [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), and [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese")) also live in Hong Kong. Southeast Asian languages such as [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") and [Malay-Indonesian](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") speaking communities also live in Hong Kong. Other minority languages with significant speakerbases in Hong Kong include [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), and [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"), among others.[[342]](#cite_note-349)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Hong Kong](/wiki/Cinema_of_Hong_Kong "Cinema of Hong Kong")

[![Statue of Bruce Lee in a fighting pose](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Bruce_Lee%2C_Avenue_of_Stars%2C_Hong_Kong_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Bruce_Lee%2C_Avenue_of_Stars%2C_Hong_Kong_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bruce_Lee,_Avenue_of_Stars,_Hong_Kong_-_panoramio.jpg)

Statue of [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee") on the [Avenue of Stars](/wiki/Avenue_of_Stars,_Hong_Kong "Avenue of Stars, Hong Kong"), a tribute to the city's film industry

Hong Kong developed into a filmmaking hub during the late 1940s as a wave of Shanghai filmmakers migrated to the territory, and these movie veterans helped build the colony's entertainment industry over the next decade.[[343]](#cite_note-350) By the 1960s, the city was well known to overseas audiences through films such as *[The World of Suzie Wong](/wiki/The_World_of_Suzie_Wong_(film) "The World of Suzie Wong (film)")*.[[344]](#cite_note-351) When [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee")'s *[The Way of the Dragon](/wiki/The_Way_of_the_Dragon "The Way of the Dragon")* released in 1972 and [Enter the Dragon](/wiki/Enter_the_Dragon "Enter the Dragon") in 1973, local productions became popular outside Hong Kong. During the 1980s and 1900s, films such as *[A Better Tomorrow](/wiki/A_Better_Tomorrow "A Better Tomorrow")*, *[As Tears Go By](/wiki/As_Tears_Go_By_(film) "As Tears Go By (film)")*, *[God of Gamblers](/wiki/God_of_Gamblers "God of Gamblers")*, *[Police Story](/wiki/Police_Story_(1985_film) "Police Story (1985 film)")* and *[Zu Warriors from the Magic Mountain](/wiki/Zu_Warriors_from_the_Magic_Mountain "Zu Warriors from the Magic Mountain")* expanded global interest beyond [martial arts films](/wiki/Martial_arts_film "Martial arts film"); locally made gangster films, romantic dramas, and supernatural fantasies became popular.[[345]](#cite_note-352)

Hong Kong cinema continued to be internationally successful over the following decades with critically acclaimed movies and dramas such as *[Farewell My Concubine](/wiki/Farewell_My_Concubine_(film) "Farewell My Concubine (film)")*, *[To Live](/wiki/To_Live_(1994_film) "To Live (1994 film)")*, *[Shaolin Soccer](/wiki/Shaolin_Soccer "Shaolin Soccer")*, *[Kung Fu Hustle](/wiki/Kung_Fu_Hustle "Kung Fu Hustle")*, *[Ip Man](/wiki/Ip_Man_(film_series) "Ip Man (film series)")* *,[Drunken Master II](/wiki/Drunken_Master_II "Drunken Master II")*, *[In the Mood for Love](/wiki/In_the_Mood_for_Love "In the Mood for Love")* and *[Chungking Express](/wiki/Chungking_Express "Chungking Express")*. The city's martial arts film roots are evident in the roles of the most prolific Hong Kong actors. [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee"), [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan"), [Stephen Chow](/wiki/Stephen_Chow "Stephen Chow"), [Jet Li](/wiki/Jet_Li "Jet Li"), [Chow Yun-fat](/wiki/Chow_Yun-fat "Chow Yun-fat"), [Donnie Yen](/wiki/Donnie_Yen "Donnie Yen") and [Michelle Yeoh](/wiki/Michelle_Yeoh "Michelle Yeoh") are some of the most popular and well known actors and actresses. Hong Kong films have also grown popular in overseas markets such as Japan, South Korea, Indonesia, Taiwan and the US, earning the city the moniker "Hollywood of the East".[[346]](#cite_note-353) At the height of the local movie industry in the early 1990s, over 400 films were produced each year; since then, industry momentum has shifted to mainland China. The number of films produced annually has declined to about 60 in 2017.[[347]](#cite_note-354)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Hong Kong](/wiki/Music_of_Hong_Kong "Music of Hong Kong")

[![Leslie Cheung with a microphone](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Leslie_Cheung_in_Madame_Tussauds_Hong_Kong_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Leslie_Cheung_in_Madame_Tussauds_Hong_Kong_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leslie_Cheung_in_Madame_Tussauds_Hong_Kong_(cropped).jpg)

[![A serious-looking Andy Lau, seated and wearing a suit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Andy_Lau_%E5%88%98%E5%BE%B7%E5%8D%8E%2C_Beijing_International_Film_Festival_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E7%94%B5%E5%BD%B1%E8%8A%82%2C_2013_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Andy_Lau_%E5%88%98%E5%BE%B7%E5%8D%8E%2C_Beijing_International_Film_Festival_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E7%94%B5%E5%BD%B1%E8%8A%82%2C_2013_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andy_Lau_%E5%88%98%E5%BE%B7%E5%8D%8E,_Beijing_International_Film_Festival_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E7%94%B5%E5%BD%B1%E8%8A%82,_2013_(cropped).jpg)

[Leslie Cheung](/wiki/Leslie_Cheung "Leslie Cheung") (left) is considered a pioneering Cantopop artist, and [Andy Lau](/wiki/Andy_Lau "Andy Lau") has been an icon of Hong Kong music and film for several decades as a member of the Four Heavenly Kings.

[Cantopop](/wiki/Cantopop "Cantopop") is a genre of Cantonese popular music which emerged in Hong Kong during the 1970s. Evolving from Shanghai-style *[shidaiqu](/wiki/Shidaiqu "Shidaiqu")*, it is also influenced by [Cantonese opera](/wiki/Cantonese_opera "Cantonese opera") and Western pop.[[348]](#cite_note-355) Local media featured songs by artists and bands such as [Sam Hui](/wiki/Sam_Hui "Sam Hui"), [Anita Mui](/wiki/Anita_Mui "Anita Mui"), [Leslie Cheung](/wiki/Leslie_Cheung "Leslie Cheung"), [Alan Tam](/wiki/Alan_Tam "Alan Tam"), [Hacken Lee](/wiki/Hacken_Lee "Hacken Lee") and [Beyond](/wiki/Beyond_(band) "Beyond (band)") ; during the 1980s, exported films and shows exposed Cantopop to a global audience.[[349]](#cite_note-356) The genre's popularity peaked in the 1990s, when the [Four Heavenly Kings](/wiki/Four_Heavenly_Kings_(Hong_Kong) "Four Heavenly Kings (Hong Kong)") dominated Asian record charts.[[350]](#cite_note-357) Despite a general decline since late in the decade,[[351]](#cite_note-358) Cantopop remains dominant in Hong Kong; contemporary artists such as [Eason Chan](/wiki/Eason_Chan "Eason Chan"), [Jackson Wang](/wiki/Jackson_Wang "Jackson Wang"), [Joey Yung](/wiki/Joey_Yung "Joey Yung"), [G.E.M.](/wiki/G.E.M. "G.E.M."), [Tyson Yoshi](/wiki/Tyson_Yoshi "Tyson Yoshi"), [Twins](/wiki/Twins_(group) "Twins (group)") and [Mirror](/wiki/Mirror_(group) "Mirror (group)") are popular in the territory and around the world.[[352]](#cite_note-359)

Western classical music has historically had a strong presence in Hong Kong and remains a large part of local musical education.[[353]](#cite_note-360) The publicly funded [Hong Kong Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Hong Kong Philharmonic Orchestra"), the territory's oldest professional symphony orchestra, and frequently hosts musicians and conductors from all over the world. The [Hong Kong Chinese Orchestra](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Chinese_Orchestra "Hong Kong Chinese Orchestra"), composed of [classical Chinese instruments](/wiki/Chinese_orchestra "Chinese orchestra"), is the leading Chinese ensemble and plays a significant role in promoting traditional music in the city.[[354]](#cite_note-361)

Hong Kong has never had a separate national anthem from the country that controlled it; its current official national anthem is therefore that of the People's Republic of China, "[March of the Volunteers](/wiki/March_of_the_Volunteers "March of the Volunteers")". The song "[Glory to Hong Kong](/wiki/Glory_to_Hong_Kong "Glory to Hong Kong")" has been used by [protestors](/wiki/2019%E2%80%932020_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–2020 Hong Kong protests") as an unofficial anthem of the city.[[355]](#cite_note-362)[[356]](#cite_note-363)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Hong Kong](/wiki/Sport_in_Hong_Kong "Sport in Hong Kong")

[![Cheering rugby fans, seen from the stands](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Crowd_cheering%2C_Hong_Kong_Sevens_2009.jpg/250px-Crowd_cheering%2C_Hong_Kong_Sevens_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowd_cheering,_Hong_Kong_Sevens_2009.jpg)

The [Hong Kong Sevens](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens"), considered the premier tournament of the [World Rugby Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Sevens Series")

The city has hosted numerous major sporting events, including the inaugural [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup"), the [2008 Summer Olympics equestrian events](/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian at the 2008 Summer Olympics"), the [2009 East Asian Games](/wiki/2009_East_Asian_Games "2009 East Asian Games"), the [Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "Rugby World Cup Sevens") in 1997 and 2005 and [2025 World Grand Prix (snooker)](/wiki/2025_World_Grand_Prix_(snooker) "2025 World Grand Prix (snooker)").[[357]](#cite_note-ShenKee-364)

The territory regularly hosts the [Hong Kong Sevens](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens"), [Hong Kong Marathon](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Marathon "Hong Kong Marathon"), [Hong Kong Open (tennis)](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Open_(tennis) "Hong Kong Open (tennis)"), [Hong Kong Tennis Classic](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Tennis_Classic "Hong Kong Tennis Classic") and [LIV Golf Hong Kong](/wiki/LIV_Golf_Hong_Kong "LIV Golf Hong Kong").

Hong Kong represents itself separately from mainland China, with its own sports teams in international competitions.[[357]](#cite_note-ShenKee-364) The territory has participated in almost every Summer Olympics since 1952 (with the exception of its support of the [1980 Summer Olympics boycott](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1980 Summer Olympics boycott")) and has earned [nine medals](/wiki/Hong_Kong_at_the_Olympics "Hong Kong at the Olympics"). [Lee Lai-shan](/wiki/Lee_Lai-shan "Lee Lai-shan") won the territory's first Olympic gold medal at the [1996 Atlanta Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics"),[[358]](#cite_note-365) and [Cheung Ka Long](/wiki/Cheung_Ka_Long "Cheung Ka Long") won the second one in [Tokyo 2020](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics").[[359]](#cite_note-366) Hong Kong athletes have won [126 medals at the Paralympic Games](/wiki/Hong_Kong_at_the_Paralympics "Hong Kong at the Paralympics") and [17 at the Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Hong_Kong_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Hong Kong at the Commonwealth Games"). No longer part of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), the city's last appearance in the latter was in [1994](/wiki/1994_Commonwealth_Games "1994 Commonwealth Games").[[360]](#cite_note-367)

Sporting leagues in Hong Kong include, [Hong Kong Premier League](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Premier_League "Hong Kong Premier League"), [Hong Kong Premiership (rugby union)](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Premiership_(rugby_union) "Hong Kong Premiership (rugby union)"), [Hong Kong A1 Division Championship](/wiki/Hong_Kong_A1_Division_Championship "Hong Kong A1 Division Championship") and the [Hong Kong Ice Hockey League](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Ice_Hockey_League "Hong Kong Ice Hockey League"). Famous local sporting clubs include [Hong Kong Football Club](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Football_Club "Hong Kong Football Club"), [South China Athletic Association](/wiki/South_China_Athletic_Association "South China Athletic Association"), [Kitchee SC](/wiki/Kitchee_SC "Kitchee SC") and [Hong Kong Eastern](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Eastern "Hong Kong Eastern").

Hong Kong sports has been described as "Club Life". Some clubs have documented their history thoroughly, while others have disappeared along with their past.

* [Hong Kong Cricket Club](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Club "Hong Kong Cricket Club")
* Craigengower Cricket Club
* Kowloon Cricket Club
* Kowloon Bowling Green Club
* Indian Recreation Club
* Chinese Recreation Club
* Filipino Club
* United Services Recreation Club
* Club de Recreio
* Club Lusitano
* [Royal Hong Kong Yacht Club](/wiki/Royal_Hong_Kong_Yacht_Club "Royal Hong Kong Yacht Club")
* Aberdeen Boat Club

[Dragon boat](/wiki/Dragon_boat "Dragon boat") races originated as a religious ceremony conducted during the annual [Tuen Ng Festival](/wiki/Dragon_Boat_Festival "Dragon Boat Festival"). The race was revived as a modern sport as part of the [Tourism Board](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Tourism_Board "Hong Kong Tourism Board")'s efforts to promote Hong Kong's image abroad. The first modern competition was organised in 1976, and overseas teams began competing in the first international race in 1993.[[361]](#cite_note-368)

The [Hong Kong Jockey Club](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Jockey_Club "Hong Kong Jockey Club"), the territory's largest taxpayer,[[362]](#cite_note-JockeyClub-369) has a monopoly on gambling and provides over 7% of government revenue.[[363]](#cite_note-370) Three forms of gambling are legal in Hong Kong: lotteries, horse racing, and football.[[362]](#cite_note-JockeyClub-369)

### Entertainment and leisure

Hong Kong has a dense mix of nightlife, live events, and major theme parks. Nightlife centres on Central, especially [Lan Kwai Fong](/wiki/Lan_Kwai_Fong "Lan Kwai Fong") (LKF), with a high concentration of bars and clubs in walkable streets. Bar culture in the city also draws global attention. [Bar Leone](/wiki/Bar_Leone "Bar Leone") was ranked No. 1 on [The World's 50 Best Bars](/wiki/The_World%27s_50_Best_Bars "The World's 50 Best Bars") in 2025. Coa was ranked Asia's Best Bar in 2021, 2022 and 2023.

For big events, [Kai Tak Sports Park](/wiki/Kai_Tak_Sports_Park "Kai Tak Sports Park") anchors the city's newest entertainment hub, built on the former airport site, with a 50,000-seat main stadium and a retractable roof. [Hong Kong Stadium](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stadium "Hong Kong Stadium") remains a key venue for football and major fixtures in Causeway Bay, with a capacity of 40,116. [AsiaWorld-Expo](/wiki/AsiaWorld-Expo "AsiaWorld-Expo") near the airport hosts concerts, exhibitions, and large-scale conventions across multiple halls and an arena-style space. [Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre "Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre") is the core waterfront venue in Wan Chai for exhibitions, meetings, and shows.

Hong Kong has two theme parks. [Hong Kong Disneyland](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Disneyland "Hong Kong Disneyland") on Lantau Island is a major theme park and resort destination. [Ocean Park Hong Kong](/wiki/Ocean_Park_Hong_Kong "Ocean Park Hong Kong") combines rides and animal exhibits, with zones across the Waterfront and Summit areas.

## Spectator sports

### Badminton

Badminton is one of the most popular sports in Hong Kong. Some of the notable current professionals includes athletes such as [Tse Ying Suet](/wiki/Tse_Ying_Suet "Tse Ying Suet")/[Tang Chun Man](/wiki/Tang_Chun_Man "Tang Chun Man") in the mixed doubles category reaching the highest world ranking of 2, and [Ng Ka Long Angus](/wiki/Ng_Ka_Long "Ng Ka Long") in the Men's single discipline with the highest ranking of 6.

### Basketball

See also: [Hong Kong men's national basketball team](/wiki/Hong_Kong_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Hong Kong men's national basketball team")

Basketball is also one of the most popular sports in Hong Kong.[[364]](#cite_note-Sport_in_Hong_Kong-371) Hong Kong's national basketball team regularly qualifies for the [FIBA Asia Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Asia_Cup "FIBA Asia Cup"). In club competitions, its team ***Regal*** won the [1997 ABC Champions Cup](/wiki/1997_ABC_Champions_Cup "1997 ABC Champions Cup").

Hong Kong hosted the official [1983 Asian Basketball Championship](/wiki/1983_ABC_Championship "1983 ABC Championship").

### Cricket

Main article: [Cricket in Hong Kong](/wiki/Cricket_in_Hong_Kong "Cricket in Hong Kong")

Cricket in Hong Kong has been played since at least 1841. Like most cricketing nations, it was part of the British Empire. The national cricket team has been active since 1866, and the [Hong Kong Cricket Association](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Association "Hong Kong Cricket Association") was granted associate membership of the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC) in 1969. Hong Kong hosted the [Hong Kong Cricket Sixes](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Sixes "Hong Kong Cricket Sixes"), an ICC sanctioned event that features teams of six players in a six over competition annually till 2012. The [Hong Kong Cricket Team](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cricket_Team "Hong Kong Cricket Team") qualified for [2014 ICC World Twenty20](/wiki/2014_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2014 ICC World Twenty20") and caused a major upset by defeating hosts Bangladesh.

### Dragon boat racing

Dragon boat racing is one of the most popular sports in Hong Kong.[[364]](#cite_note-Sport_in_Hong_Kong-371) The traditional Chinese sport of [dragon boat](/wiki/Dragon_boat "Dragon boat") racing was originally held as part of the [Duan Wu festivities](/wiki/Duan_Wu_festival "Duan Wu festival") in Hong Kong. It is now spread internationally with races held all around the world. The Hong Kong Dragon Boat Association was a founding member of the international and regional governing bodies for dragon boat racing: The International Dragon Boat Federation and Asian Dragon Boat Federation. The oldest International Festival Races are those held in Hong Kong annually. The HKIR have been held since 1976 and are acknowledged as starting the modern era of the dragon boatsport.

### Football

Main article: [Football in Hong Kong](/wiki/Football_in_Hong_Kong "Football in Hong Kong")

Football is one of the most popular sports in Hong Kong, with [Hong Kong Premier League](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Premier_League "Hong Kong Premier League") being the only fully professional sports league in the city.[[364]](#cite_note-Sport_in_Hong_Kong-371) [Lunar New Year Cup](/wiki/Lunar_New_Year_Cup "Lunar New Year Cup"), which is held in [Hong Kong Stadium](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stadium "Hong Kong Stadium"), is a famous international football tournament hosted in Hong Kong.

### Golf

Golf has been played in Hong Kong ever since the establishment of the Hong Kong Golf Club in 1879. The club, which has hosted the Hong Kong Open every year since 1959, has two sites - Fanling, which is home to three championship-calibre courses, and Deep Water Bay, a short nine hole course in southern Hong Kong Island. Hong Kong Golf Club is considered among the premier golf clubs in Asia and, despite being a private members' club, is open to visitors from Mondays through Fridays.

### Horse racing

Horse racing is by far the most popular spectator sport in Hong Kong and generates the largest [horse race gambling](/wiki/Betting_on_horse_racing "Betting on horse racing") turnover in the world. [British tradition](/wiki/Horseracing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Horseracing in the United Kingdom") left its mark as one of the most important entertainment and gambling institutions in Hong Kong. Established as the Royal [Hong Kong Jockey Club](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Jockey_Club "Hong Kong Jockey Club") in 1884, the non-profit organization conducts nearly 700 races every season at the two race tracks in [Happy Valley](/wiki/Happy_Valley_Racecourse "Happy Valley Racecourse") and [Sha Tin](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Racecourse "Sha Tin Racecourse"). The sport annually draws in over 11% of Hong Kong's tax revenue. Off-track betting is available from overseas bookmakers. In 2009, betting on horse races generated an average US$12.7 million in gambling turnover per race, 6 times larger than its closest rival [France](/wiki/France "France") at US$2 million while the United States only generated $250,000. Some consider betting on horse races an investment.

### Lacrosse

[Hong Kong men's national lacrosse team](/wiki/Hong_Kong_men%27s_national_lacrosse_team "Hong Kong men's national lacrosse team") is one of Asia's top teams. It has qualified for the [World Lacrosse Championship](/wiki/World_Lacrosse_Championship "World Lacrosse Championship") for the fifth consecutive time.

Hong Kong has sent national teams to the [Under-19 World Lacrosse Championships](/wiki/Under-19_World_Lacrosse_Championships "Under-19 World Lacrosse Championships").[[365]](#cite_note-372)

In October 2021, Hong Kong's women's lacrosse team won bronze Dalmatia Cup in [Split, Croatia](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia"). In its preparation for the [World Lacrosse Women's World Championship](/wiki/World_Lacrosse_Women%27s_World_Championship "World Lacrosse Women's World Championship"), Hong Kong beat the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_women%27s_national_lacrosse_team "Czech Republic women's national lacrosse team") 8–4 as team captain Daphne Li won the tournament [MVP](/wiki/Most_Valuable_Player "Most Valuable Player") award. Head coach was Jenifer Marrosu.

### Rugby union

Main article: [Rugby union in Hong Kong](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Hong_Kong "Rugby union in Hong Kong")

The [Hong Kong Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Rugby_Football_Union "Hong Kong Rugby Football Union") was founded in 1953.

Rugby is long established, partly as a result of its being a British colony. In contrast to the People's Republic of China, it has had a continuous existence dating back over a hundred years.

[Hong Kong Sevens](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens") is held annually in Hong Kong and is considered the premier tournament on the [World Rugby Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Sevens Series") competition.

[Hong Kong national rugby union team](/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_rugby_union_team "Hong Kong national rugby union team") is ranked 23rd in the world with a peak rating of 21st (2018–2022).

Hong Kong is one of the best teams in Asia and has finished first place in the [Asia Rugby Championship](/wiki/Asia_Rugby_Championship "Asia Rugby Championship") in 2018, 2019, 2022, 2023, 2024 and 2025.

They qualified for the [2027 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2027_Men%27s_Rugby_World_Cup "2027 Men's Rugby World Cup") for the first time by winning the [2025 Asia Rugby Championship](/wiki/2025_Asia_Rugby_Championship "2025 Asia Rugby Championship").

The [Hong Kong national rugby sevens team](/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_rugby_sevens_team "Hong Kong national rugby sevens team") is widely reguarded as the best [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") team in Asia and regular participant in the [Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "Rugby World Cup Sevens"). Hong Kong has won the [Asian Sevens Series](/wiki/Asian_Sevens_Series "Asian Sevens Series") a record 8 times and the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games") 2 times.

## Education

Main article: [Education in Hong Kong](/wiki/Education_in_Hong_Kong "Education in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Bishop%27s_House%2C_Anglican_Church%2C_Hong_Kong.JPG/250px-Bishop%27s_House%2C_Anglican_Church%2C_Hong_Kong.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bishop%27s_House,_Anglican_Church,_Hong_Kong.JPG)

Old campus of [St. Paul's College](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_College,_Hong_Kong "St. Paul's College, Hong Kong"), the first school established in the colonial era

Education in Hong Kong is managed primarily by the [Hong Kong Education Bureau](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Education_Bureau "Hong Kong Education Bureau") and is largely modelled on [that of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_Kingdom "Education in the United Kingdom"), particularly the [English system](/wiki/Education_in_England "Education in England").[[366]](#cite_note-373) Children are required to attend school from age 6 until the completion of secondary education, generally at age 18.[[367]](#cite_note-nss-374)[[368]](#cite_note-HKEduLi-375) At the end of secondary schooling, local school students take a public examination and are awarded the [Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Diploma_of_Secondary_Education "Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education") upon successful completion of the exam.[[369]](#cite_note-376)

Of residents aged 15 and older, 81% completed lower-secondary education, 66% graduated from an upper secondary school, 32% attended a non-degree tertiary program, and 24% earned a bachelor's degree or higher.[[370]](#cite_note-377)

Mandatory education has contributed to an adult literacy rate of 95.7%.[[371]](#cite_note-378) This literacy rate is lower than that of other developed economies because of the influx of refugees from mainland China during the post-war colonial era; much of the elderly population were not formally educated because of war and poverty.[[372]](#cite_note-379)[[373]](#cite_note-380)

Comprehensive schools fall into three categories: government-run public schools; subsidised schools, including government-aided and grant schools; and private schools, often run by religious organisations, that base admissions on academic merit. These schools are subject to the Education Bureau's curriculum guidelines. Private schools subsidised under the [Direct Subsidy Scheme](/wiki/Direct_Subsidy_Scheme "Direct Subsidy Scheme"), and international schools fall outside of this system and may elect to use differing curricula and teach using other languages.[[368]](#cite_note-HKEduLi-375)

Hong Kong students perform exceptionally well in global benchmarks. In [PISA](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") 2022 (latest available results), Hong Kong ranked in the top tier worldwide; 4th in mathematics and top 10 in reading and science. This places it well above the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") average and among the highest-performing systems globally.[[374]](#cite_note-381)

Schools in Hong Kong offer a wide range of curriculums, the [Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Diploma_of_Secondary_Education "Hong Kong Diploma of Secondary Education"), [International Baccalaureate](/wiki/International_Baccalaureate "International Baccalaureate"), [GCE A-level](/wiki/GCE_A-level "GCE A-level"), [IGCSEs](/wiki/IGCSEs "IGCSEs") and [Advanced Placement](/wiki/Advanced_Placement "Advanced Placement") are most popular.

Hong Kong Schools offer world-class education with top-tier, high-ranking schools such as [St Paul's Co-educational College](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Co-educational_College "St Paul's Co-educational College") ranked 2nd in Global Top IB Schools 2025 with a 42.4 average score. While schools such as [Diocesan Boys' School](/wiki/Diocesan_Boys%27_School "Diocesan Boys' School") and [German Swiss International School](/wiki/German_Swiss_International_School "German Swiss International School") respectively also rank within the top 10.[[375]](#cite_note-382)

[German Swiss International School](/wiki/German_Swiss_International_School "German Swiss International School") 91% of all grades awarded to Year 11 students were A\*/A or 9/8/7 level.

[Hong Kong International School](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_School "Hong Kong International School") has exceptional [AP](/wiki/Advanced_Placement "Advanced Placement") results, In 2025, 80% of students earned scores of 4 or 5. The school follows a US-style curriculum and prepares students for US universities.

There are also many overseas British independent schools in Hong Kong such as [Harrow International School Hong Kong](/wiki/Harrow_International_School_Hong_Kong "Harrow International School Hong Kong") where 90% of students received (A\*-B), Wycombe Abbey School Hong Kong, Malvern College Hong Kong and Shrewsbury International School Hong Kong.

Moreover, there are many schools that cover various regions of the world such as; [Australian International School](/wiki/Australian_International_School_Hong_Kong "Australian International School Hong Kong"), American International School, [Canadian International School](/wiki/Canadian_International_School_of_Hong_Kong "Canadian International School of Hong Kong"), [French International School](/wiki/French_International_School_of_Hong_Kong "French International School of Hong Kong"), Korean International School among others.

Local schools in Hong Kong are categorized into three "bands" based on academic performance, with Band 1 schools being the most prestigious and competitive. These institutions are often criticized for being high-pressure environments. Band 1 schools include [Diocesan Boys' School](/wiki/Diocesan_Boys%27_School "Diocesan Boys' School"), [Diocesan Girls' School](/wiki/Diocesan_Girls%27_School "Diocesan Girls' School"), [Ying Wa College](/wiki/Ying_Wa_College "Ying Wa College"), [St. Paul's College](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_College,_Hong_Kong "St. Paul's College, Hong Kong") and [La Salle College](/wiki/La_Salle_College "La Salle College").

### Medium of instruction

At primary and secondary school levels, the government maintains a policy of "mother tongue instruction"; most schools use Cantonese as the [medium of instruction](/wiki/Medium_of_instruction "Medium of instruction"), with written education in both Chinese and English. Other languages being used as the medium of instruction in non-international school education include English and [Putonghua](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") (Standard Mandarin Chinese). Secondary schools emphasise "bi-literacy and tri-lingualism", which has encouraged the proliferation of spoken Mandarin language education.[[376]](#cite_note-383)

English is the official medium of instruction and assessment for most university programmes in Hong Kong, although Cantonese is predominant in informal discussions among local students and professors.[[377]](#cite_note-384)[[378]](#cite_note-385)[[379]](#cite_note-386)[[380]](#cite_note-387)[[381]](#cite_note-388)[[382]](#cite_note-389)

### Tertiary education

Main article: [Higher education in Hong Kong](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Hong_Kong "Higher education in Hong Kong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Main_Building_HKU_20100926_03.JPG/250px-Main_Building_HKU_20100926_03.JPG)](/wiki/File:Main_Building_HKU_20100926_03.JPG)

[University of Hong Kong](/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") main building

Hong Kong has twelve universities. The [University of Hong Kong](/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") (HKU) ranked [QS Asia #1](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") was founded as the city's first institute of higher education in 1911 during the early colonial period.[[383]](#cite_note-390) The [Chinese University of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chinese_University_of_Hong_Kong "Chinese University of Hong Kong") (#7 Asia Ranking) was established in 1963 to fill the need for a university that taught using Chinese as its primary language of instruction.[[384]](#cite_note-391) Along with the [Hong Kong University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Hong_Kong_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Hong Kong University of Science and Technology") (#6) established in 1991, these universities are consistently ranked among the top 50 or top 100 universities worldwide.[[385]](#cite_note-:1-392)[[386]](#cite_note-:2-393)[[387]](#cite_note-:3-394)

The [Hong Kong Polytechnic University](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Polytechnic_University "Hong Kong Polytechnic University") (PolyU)[[388]](#cite_note-395) and [City University of Hong Kong](/wiki/City_University_of_Hong_Kong "City University of Hong Kong") (CityU), both granted university status in 1994, are consistently ranked among the top 100 or top 200 universities worldwide.[[385]](#cite_note-:1-392)[[386]](#cite_note-:2-393)[[387]](#cite_note-:3-394) The [Hong Kong Baptist University](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Baptist_University "Hong Kong Baptist University") (HKBU) was granted university status in 1994[[389]](#cite_note-396) and is a liberal arts institution. [Hong Kong Metropolitan University](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Metropolitan_University "Hong Kong Metropolitan University") (formerly as Open University of Hong Kong before 2021) (attaining status in 1997),[[390]](#cite_note-397) [Lingnan University](/wiki/Lingnan_University "Lingnan University") (in 1999),[[391]](#cite_note-398) [Hong Kong Shue Yan University](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Shue_Yan_University "Hong Kong Shue Yan University") (in 2006),[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELegCo_Grant_to_Shue_Yan_University2007-399) [Education University of Hong Kong](/wiki/Education_University_of_Hong_Kong "Education University of Hong Kong") (in 2016), [Hang Seng University of Hong Kong](/wiki/Hang_Seng_University_of_Hong_Kong "Hang Seng University of Hong Kong") (in 2018) and [Saint Francis University](/wiki/Saint_Francis_University_(Hong_Kong) "Saint Francis University (Hong Kong)") (in 2024)[[393]](#cite_note-400) all attained full university status in subsequent years.

Overseas universities in Hong Kong often operate as branch campuses or partnerships offering accredited degrees, such as the [University of Chicago Hong Kong](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Hong_Kong "University of Chicago Hong Kong"), [University of Sunderland](/wiki/University_of_Sunderland "University of Sunderland"), [University of Wollongong](/wiki/University_of_Wollongong "University of Wollongong") in Hong Kong. These institutions, provide local and internationally recognized qualifications.

In 2026, [QS](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") Best Student Cities ranked Hong Kong as the 17th best city for university students. 7th in best Asia for best student cities. Noting, high scores in employer activity, desirability, and diverse student population.

## Healthcare

Main article: [Health in Hong Kong](/wiki/Health_in_Hong_Kong "Health in Hong Kong")

Hong Kong is one of the healthiest places in the world. Because of its early health education, professional health services, and well-developed health care and medication system.

Public healthcare in Hong Kong is recognized for its high-quality medical outcomes. The territory has [highest life expectancy in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy") at 85.9 years. Hong Kong has one of the [lowest infant mortality rates in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_infant_mortality_rate "List of countries by infant mortality rate"), with recent estimates placing it around 1.7 to 2.5 deaths per 1,000 live births. However the public system also faces challenges, including high patient volumes, overcrowding in emergency departments, and long wait times for specialist appointments and elective procedures.

Healthcare in Hong Kong is provided through two categories consisting of public and private sectors. While private healthcare services cater to patients seeking shorter wait times and personalized care, the majority of the population relies on the extensive public healthcare system. Public hospitals and clinics are heavily subsidized by the government, ensuring affordable access to a wide range of medical services.

Hong Kong's healthcare system is managed primarily by the [Hospital Authority](/wiki/Hospital_Authority "Hospital Authority"), which was established in 1990 to oversee and operate all public hospitals in the region. The system is organized into seven geographic clusters that group hospitals and associated healthcare facilities based on their distribution across the territory. As of 2026, Hong Kong has a total of 42 public hospitals and 11 private hospitals.

The [University of Hong Kong](/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") (HKU) [Li Ka Shing Faculty of Medicine](/wiki/Li_Ka_Shing_Faculty_of_Medicine "Li Ka Shing Faculty of Medicine") is consistently ranked among the top medical schools globally and is a leader in Asia, ranking 18th worldwide in the [Times Higher Education](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings") 2026 Rankings for Clinical and Health and 24th in the QS World University Rankings Medicine.

## Media

Main article: [Media of Hong Kong](/wiki/Media_of_Hong_Kong "Media of Hong Kong")

[![Modern, green-and-white building with dish antennas on top](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Tvbcity-s1.png/250px-Tvbcity-s1.png)](/wiki/File:Tvbcity-s1.png)

[TVB City](/wiki/TVB_City "TVB City"), headquarters of Hong Kong's first over-the-air television station

Most newspapers in Hong Kong are written in Chinese. Still, there are also a few English-language newspapers, the major one being the *[South China Morning Post](/wiki/South_China_Morning_Post "South China Morning Post")*, with *[The Standard](/wiki/The_Standard_(Hong_Kong) "The Standard (Hong Kong)")* serving as a business-oriented alternative. A variety of Chinese-language newspapers are published daily; the most prominent are *[Ming Pao](/wiki/Ming_Pao "Ming Pao")* and *[Oriental Daily News](/wiki/Oriental_Daily_News "Oriental Daily News")*. Local publications are often politically affiliated, with pro-Beijing or pro-democracy sympathies. The central government has a print-media presence in the territory through the state-owned *[Ta Kung Pao](/wiki/Ta_Kung_Pao "Ta Kung Pao")* and *[Wen Wei Po](/wiki/Wen_Wei_Po "Wen Wei Po")*.[[394]](#cite_note-401) Several international publications have regional operations in Hong Kong, including *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*, *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")*, the *[Yomiuri Shimbun](/wiki/Yomiuri_Shimbun "Yomiuri Shimbun")*, and *[The Nikkei](/wiki/The_Nikkei "The Nikkei")*.[[395]](#cite_note-402)

Four [free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") television broadcasters operate in the territory; [TVB](/wiki/TVB "TVB"), [HKTVE](/wiki/HK_Television_Entertainment "HK Television Entertainment"), [HOY](/wiki/I-CABLE_HOY_Limited "I-CABLE HOY Limited") and [Phoenix Television](/wiki/Phoenix_Television "Phoenix Television") air eight [digital](/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") channels.[[396]](#cite_note-broadcasterList-403)[[397]](#cite_note-404) TVB, Hong Kong's dominant television network, has an 80% viewer share.[[398]](#cite_note-405) [Paid television services](/wiki/Television_in_Hong_Kong "Television in Hong Kong") operated by [PCCW](/wiki/PCCW "PCCW") offer hundreds of additional channels and cater to a variety of audiences.[[399]](#cite_note-406) [RTHK](/wiki/RTHK "RTHK") is the public broadcaster, providing seven radio channels and six free-to-air television channels.[[400]](#cite_note-407)[[401]](#cite_note-408)[[402]](#cite_note-409) Ten non-domestic broadcasters air programming for the territory's foreign population.[[396]](#cite_note-broadcasterList-403) Access to media and information over the Internet is not subject to mainland Chinese regulations, including the [Great Firewall](/wiki/Great_Firewall "Great Firewall"), but [local internet censorship](/wiki/Internet_censorship_in_Hong_Kong "Internet censorship in Hong Kong") applies.[[403]](#cite_note-410)

96. **[^](#cite_ref-Presidential_system_102-0)** Baohui Zhang. ["Presidential vs Parliamentary democracies: Implication for Hong Kong's Democratization"](https://cpsa-acsp.ca/papers-2006/Zhang.pdf) (PDF). [Lingnan University](/wiki/Lingnan_University "Lingnan University"). "The institutional weaknesses of the current constitutional order of Hong Kong is derived from the Basic Law, which intends to elevate the Chief Executive above partisan politics so that he only represents the people of Hong Kong. Therefore, although not specifically stipulated in the Basic Law, the non-partisan spirit embodied by the law has resulted in the established practice that the Chief Executive should not have affiliations to political parties. This means that the current political system of Hong Kong has the form of a presidential system, which means the chief executive and the legislature are separately chosen and are politically independently from each other, but not the necessary political requisites to make the system function properly."
